interactions.summary: Jiang warns poor or non-powerful students that the Ivy League can make them arrogant, utilitarian, narrow, anti-failure, and unable to build real skills. He uses his own post-Yale depression and basement years as evidence that he had to relearn openness, failure, resilience, and learning.
A student asks whether one can be open-minded, get good grades, and become rich at the same time.

Jiang begins with two modes, altruistic and utilitarian, then corrects a student’s objection about psychology by saying the model is useful rather than absolute. He argues altruistic connection produces creativity and love while utilitarian reward focus is mutually exclusive with it.
A student challenges Jiang by saying he previously called psychology brainwashing.

claims.claim: For poor students without family power, Yale or Harvard can be destructive because the system trains arrogance, utilitarian calculation, narrowness, fear of failure, and inability to learn deeply.
Jiang treats his own post-Yale collapse, depression, suicidal thoughts, video-game withdrawal, and later teaching as evidence that Yale traumatized him and that he had to relearn real learning.
His success formula is open-mindedness, acceptance of failure, resilience, and commitment to learning, not Harvard, Yale, or Princeton.

Altruistic and utilitarian modes are mutually exclusive in Jiang’s model: altruistic connection supports creativity, passion, and love, while utilitarian reward-focus pursues grades and cannot simultaneously help the world.

signature_moments.moment: The Ivy League is good for the already rich because it trains them into power, but it can ruin the poor person who needs real skills and passion.
signature_moments.source_phrase: great system if you come from a rich family
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It makes the critique class-specific instead of generically anti-school.

signature_moments.moment: Yale sends Jiang into failure, depression, video games, and his parents’ basement before he can rebuild himself as a teacher.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It is the most personal proof point in the episode.

signature_moments.moment: The student asks for both grades and goodness, and Jiang answers that reward focus and creative altruism cannot occupy the same mode.
signature_moments.source_phrase: impossible to both want to have really good grades as well as help the world
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It gives the final Q&A its psychological trap.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Fight-or-flight reward focus oriented toward grades, wealth, and strategic advantage.
A connection-oriented mode associated with family, friendship, creativity, passion, and love.
The willingness to ask questions, learn from others, accept failure, and grow.
